results.possibleIdentity: Joseph E. Japka was a metallurgical engineer active in the late 1960s through the 1990s, primarily based in Highland Park and New Brunswick, New Jersey. In the late 1960s and early 1970s, he worked for the Thomas & Betts Company as a metallurgical engineer, where he developed and patented various electrical connection technologies, including methods for making terminals for flat cables and electrical connectors. During this time, he was associated with Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.), a non-profit organization that facilitated collaborations between the artistic and technical communities. His early technical contributions include co-authoring the 1967 NASA technical report 'Metallurgical Evaluation of Iron-Base Alloys for Use as Gas-Pressure Bonding Vessels' with Emanuel Gordon. Later in his career, Japka became a key technical figure at Procedyne Corporation in New Brunswick, New Jersey, where he served in management and engineering roles. At Procedyne, he focused on fluidized bed furnace technology, heat treating processes, and surface coatings for metals, holding multiple patents in these fields and publishing research on automated heat treating and the properties of iron powders.
